topic: Two friends plan separate birthday parties on the same day
raw_output:
[story 1]

The calendar glowed accusingly on Liam’s desk. Across the room, Maya was laughing, her voice bright and carefree, discussing decorations for her garden bash. Liam traced the grain of the wooden surface, his knuckles white. He kept replaying the conversation—the careful scheduling, the deliberate separation. A cold knot tightened in his stomach, a heavy, suffocating pressure that made breathing shallow. He felt a strange, hollow ache where excitement should have been. Every time Maya mentioned the shared date, a sharp, unpleasant jolt ran down his spine, leaving him wanting to retreat entirely. He stood up abruptly, needing air, the silence of his apartment suddenly amplifying the frantic drumming in his chest.

[story 2]

“So, the theme is tropical, right?” Maya chirped, completely oblivious to the tension radiating off Alex. Alex managed a tight smile, nodding mechanically. Inside, a tremor ran through him, subtle but undeniable, like a plucked, taut string. He watched her animated gestures, cataloging every detail, trying to anchor himself to the surface level of their camaraderie. When she mentioned the logistics of the venue, Alex’s hands began to shake slightly, a tremor he couldn't suppress. He found himself focusing intensely on a chipped coffee mug, the warmth doing nothing to soothe the internal chill that had settled deep in his core. The thought of them celebrating separately, on the very same day, felt like a deliberate, sharp cut.

[story 3]

I kept checking my phone, the screen illuminating my face in the dim light of the kitchen. The plans were laid out, meticulously divided. Maya’s party was loud, vibrant, full of shared history. Mine was quiet, solitary, a carefully constructed bubble of isolation. A tightness constricted my throat, making swallowing feel like a physical effort. I stared out the window at the streetlights, watching the world move on without me. Every casual mention of the impending date sent a wave of nausea through me. I paced the length of the rug, my steps quick and uneven, unable to settle. The air felt too thick, too heavy, pressing down on my chest until I had to stop and take several ragged, deliberate breaths just to keep the panic from escalating.
